attributes.stem: A 64-year-old man presents with a six-month history of a painless abdominal swelling, which is slowly increasing in size and is most noticeable when he stands up. He reports no weight loss or change in bowel habits. He has a medical history of chronic Hepatitis C infection. On examination, his BMI is 27 kg/m2 and he has a moderately distended abdomen. The abdomen is soft to palpation and shifting dullness can be demonstrated. The swelling is clearly evident (Image).
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This is a large paraumbilical hernia.  The patient has ascites (as shown by the presence of shifting dullness) and when he stands up the hernia fills with ascites to such an extent that the overlying skin has started to ulcerate.  These hernias are very common, particularly in the obese or in any condition where there is chronically elevated intra-abdominal pressure - such as liver failure and ascites.  Paraumbilical hernias appear as crescent shaped masses and are due to defects through the linea alba in the region of the umbilicus, usually related to diastasis of the rectus abdominis muscles. The neck of the defect is often narrow and any contents of the hernial sac at risk of strangulation.  Repair of the defect should be advised - together with treatment of the underlying cause.

A Sister Mary Joseph nodule is a metastatic deposit in the umbilicus, with the primary site is most often in the gastrointestinal tract (stomach, pancreas, colon), but could be gynaecological or breast in origin. The presence of this sign usually indicates a poor prognosis, with widespread intraperitoneal disease. Clinically important weight loss is generally defined as loss of more than five percent of usual body weight over 6-12 months.

Omphalitis is an infection of the umbilical cord stump and/or surrounding tissues, characterised by purulent (±bloody) discharge from the umbilicus with surrounding induration, erythema, and tenderness. Systemic signs such as fever, lethargy or poor feeding are suggestive of a more severe infection. Omphalitis is typically seen in neonates.

Ectopia vesica also known as exstrophy of the bladder is a rare congenital defect, where the urinary bladder herniates through an anterior abdominal wall defect, which is usually surgically corrected in the neonatal period. Their malignant potential is considerable (mostly adenocarcinomas), but for them to present in an adult is exceedingly rare.

A lipoma is a benign tumour comprised of fat tissue. They typically are a small, soft, rubbery lump located just beneath the skin which are painless and most often found on the upper back, shoulders, arms, buttocks, and upper thighs. They do not usually change once they are formed.
learningPoints: Paraumbilical hernia are common in the obese or in any condition where there is chronically elevated intra-abdominal pressure.
Paraumbilical hernias appear as crescent shaped masses in the umbilical region and due to weak points between the umbilical scar and the linea alba.
references: Aguirre DA, Santosa AC, Casola G, Sirlin CB. Abdominal wall hernias: imaging features, complications, and diagnostic pitfalls at multi-detector row CT. Radiographics. 2005;25(6):1501-20. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/16284131/

Brooks D. Overview of abdominal wall hernias in adults. In: Uptodate [online]. 2022. Available at: https://www.uptodate.com/contents/overview-of-abdominal-wall-hernias-in-adults#:~:text=A%20hernia%20is%20a%20protrusion,by%20a%20surgeon%20when%20identified

Eldirdiri S, Elmushly RM, Elazhary SG. Late presentation of ectopia vesica with malignant transformation. A case report and review. Int J Surg Case Rep. 2018;48:43-46. Available at: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC6028663/

Ota S, Haruyama T, Ishihara M, Natsume M, Fukasawa Y, Sakamoto T, et al. A Patient with Advanced Gastric Cancer Who Achieved a Long-Term Prognosis by Early Diagnosis of Sister Mary Joseph's Nodule. Case Rep Oncol. 2018;11(1):11-16. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/29515403/
